content.text: Based on the official documentation and industry best practices for long-running agent workflows,
"24/7 agent hosting patterns (VPS / cloud run / container)"
three primary infrastructure options for running AI agents continuously without interruption
— each with different trade-offs in cost, control, scalability, and operational complexity for production agent deployments.

attrs.alt: 24/7 agent hosting patterns (VPS / cloud run / container) diagram

content.text: VPS is a dedicated virtual machine
where your agent runs in its own isolated VM, sandboxed from your workstation.
Agent runs in own VM, isolated from SSH keys, browser sessions, personal files
Close laptop and agent keeps going; sessions persist across disconnects
Offload heavy lifting; laptop stays cool for keyboard work
Run coding agent overnight for long refactors
Orchestrate AI agent teams from single VPS
Let agent run autonomously without worry
Your laptop stays clean, files stay safe

# Pick a Linux VPS plan
# Install agent dependencies
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3 pip docker

# Run agent
docker run ai-agent-247
: "Pick a Linux VPS plan and have your agent running in minutes".
Cloud Run is a fully managed serverless platform
that enables you to run code directly on Google's scalable infrastructure.
Deploys container image, not files directly
Use Second generation execution environment for reduced latency

# Required settings for Cloud Run
- Container Image URL: openpolicyagent/opa:<version>
- CPU is always allocated: true          # Prevent delayed startup
- Minimum number of instances: 1         # Keep service ready
- Execution environment: Second generation
- Container Port: 8181
- Container Arguments: run --server --addr=:8181 --config-file=/run/secrets/secret
: "CPU is always allocated: true" — without this, decisions delayed while service starts causing bad user experience.
Centralized PDP called from PEPs running elsewhere
Run as sidecar to other application containers
Multi-agent private networking patterns for root agents + subagents
Cloud handles infrastructure, you focus on delivering apps
Automatic scaling with stateless container model
Fully managed — no infrastructure to maintain
Full control over networking, scaling, security
Root agents and subagents in private networks
Run on local servers to enhance privacy
Cloud services expensive; local has predictable costs
: "While cloud services can become quite expensive quickly, running processes locally demands greater initial investment in hardware".
Runs overnight, sessions persist across disconnects
: You have your own space (VM), can decorate however you want (full OS access), but you don't own the building. Your agent lives there 24/7, and you just check in when needed.
: The hotel handles everything (scaling, networking), you just show up (send request) and leave. Perfect for short visits but not for living there 24/7.
: You control everything (infrastructure), but you have to build and maintain it yourself. Great if you need specific features, but expensive and requires work.
VPS: dedicated VM, sandboxed, runs 24/7, sessions persist, €5/month minimum
Cloud Run: fully managed serverless, stateless containers, auto-scaling, pay per request
Container: self-hosted on Kubernetes/Docker, full infrastructure control, requires hardware investment
VPS best for: Claude Code 24/7, autonomous work, multi-agent teams from single server
Cloud Run best for: API endpoints, policy decision points, bursty workloads, scalable tasks
Container best for: privacy-critical, latency-sensitive, custom orchestration needs
Cloud Run requires: CPU always allocated=true, minimum instances=1, second generation
VPS benefits: sandboxed, runs while you sleep, keeps laptop clean
Cloud Run benefits: no server management, seamless scalability, focus on app value
Container trade-offs: high control, privacy, lower latency vs. high cost, maintenance burden
Cloud services can become expensive quickly, but local infrastructure requires initial hardware investment — choose based on privacy needs, latency requirements, and scalability demands.
Understanding the internals of 24/7 agent hosting patterns (vps / cloud run / container) helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with 24/7 agent hosting patterns (vps / cloud run / container) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying 24/7 agent hosting patterns (vps / cloud run / container) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

24/7 agent hosting patterns (VPS / cloud run / container) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

24/7 agent hosting patterns (VPS / cloud run / container) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Health checks are the mechanism by which your container orchestrator (Docker, Kubernetes, Cloud Run) determines whether an agent instance is fit to receive work. Without health checks, a container that has silently deadlocked or run out of memory continues to receive requests until manually restarted, causing prolonged outages. Modern container platforms distinguish three probe types: liveness (is the process alive?), readiness (is it ready to accept requests?), and startup (has it finished initializing?). Each serves a distinct purpose, and conflating them causes subtle production issues — for example, using a liveness probe that hits an expensive initialization path causes the orchestrator to repeatedly restart a container that is actually healthy but slow to start.

For long-running agent containers, the readiness probe is the most important. An agent that has consumed its API rate limit budget is alive (liveness passes) but cannot meaningfully process new tasks (readiness should fail). Implementing readiness as a separate endpoint that checks the agent's actual capacity — remaining rate limit, available memory, connection to downstream services — allows the orchestrator to route work away from an overloaded instance without restarting it. The startup probe with a generous initialStartupPeriod prevents premature liveness failures during cold starts, which are common for agents that load large model files or establish database connection pools at startup.
Liveness probe: GET /health/live — returns 200 if the process is running and not deadlocked
Readiness probe: GET /health/ready — returns 200 only when able to accept new tasks
Startup probe: allow 60-120s for cold starts before enabling liveness checks
Readiness should check: rate limit budget, memory headroom, database connectivity
Health endpoint must respond in < 100ms — never call external APIs from a health check
Return structured JSON: {status, checks: {db, rate_limit, memory}} for debuggability
Set failureThreshold >= 3 to tolerate transient failures without unnecessary restarts
Expose /health/metrics for Prometheus scraping alongside the readiness endpoint

Auto-scaling determines how many instances of your agent container run in response to load. For request-driven agents on Cloud Run, the platform handles scaling automatically based on concurrent requests — adding instances when demand spikes and scaling back to zero during idle periods. For always-on agents on VPS or Kubernetes, you must configure scaling explicitly using Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) rules that define the metrics triggering scale-out (CPU utilization, queue depth, active task count) and the minimum/maximum replica bounds. The minimum instance count is a critical decision: setting it to zero eliminates idle costs but introduces cold start latency; setting it to 1 or 2 keeps instances warm at the cost of always paying for those instances.

For long-running agent workloads, CPU utilization is a poor scaling signal because agents spend most of their time waiting for API responses (low CPU) while consuming significant resources. Better signals are active task count (scale when each instance is handling more than N concurrent tasks) and queue depth (scale when the pending-work queue exceeds a threshold). On Kubernetes, KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) supports queue-based scaling with direct integrations for RabbitMQ, Kafka, and Redis queues — making it the preferred choice for agent task queues. Set scaleDown stabilizationWindowSeconds to at least 300 seconds to prevent flapping during bursty workloads.
Cloud Run: set min-instances=1 for low-latency agents, 0 for cost-optimized batch agents
Kubernetes HPA: scale on active_tasks_per_pod metric rather than CPU for agent workloads
KEDA: use queue-depth scaling for Kafka/Redis task queues — scale exactly to queue load
scaleDown stabilizationWindowSeconds: set 300s minimum to prevent flapping
Set maxReplicas: cap instances to avoid runaway Claude API costs during traffic spikes
Implement graceful shutdown: drain active tasks before termination on scale-down
VPS: use systemd with autorestart, or a process manager like Supervisor for resilience
Load test before go-live: confirm scale-out happens fast enough for your latency SLA

# Kubernetes HPA configuration for long-running agent pods
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: claude-agent-hpa
  namespace: agents
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: claude-agent
  minReplicas: 2       # always-warm for < 100ms cold start
  maxReplicas: 20      # cap to control Claude API spend
  metrics:
    - type: Pods
      pods:
        metric:
          name: active_tasks_per_pod   # custom metric from /health/metrics
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: "5"            # scale when avg tasks/pod exceeds 5
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 75
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300  # wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 1
          periodSeconds: 60             # remove 1 pod per minute max
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0    # scale up immediately
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 4
          periodSeconds: 30             # add up to 4 pods per 30s
---
# Cloud Run service with min-instances for low latency
apiVersion: serving.knative.dev/v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: claude-agent-cloudrun
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        autoscaling.knative.dev/minScale: "1"   # keep 1 warm instance
        autoscaling.knative.dev/maxScale: "100"  # cap for cost control
        run.googleapis.com/cpu-throttling: "false"  # always allocate CPU
